topic.what: Proposed the multistep cascade model of gastric carcinogenesis from normal mucosa through gastritis, atrophy, intestinal metaplasia, and dysplasia to cancer.
topic.how: Synthesis of epidemiological, pathological, and clinical evidence into a unified progressive model with identifiable intermediate stages.
topic.why_matters: The Correa cascade is the conceptual backbone of all gastric cancer prevention — it explains why chronic gastritis matters and defines the surveillance targets.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Whether the cascade stages predict cancer risk
characteristics.value: Validated by Uemura 2001 prospective cohort and multiple subsequent studies

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree of adoption in clinical guidelines
characteristics.value: Foundational to all major guidelines (MAPS, AGA, Maastricht)
characteristics.evidence: Cited in all major gastric cancer prevention guidelines

novelty: First unified multistep model of gastric carcinogenesis
Identified reversible and irreversible stages in the progression
Linked infectious (H. pylori), dietary (salt, nitrosamines), and genetic factors into one framework
Defined 'point of no return' concept for cancer prevention
open_problems: Exact 'point of no return' for H. pylori eradication benefit remains debated
Model applies to intestinal-type cancer; diffuse-type has different pathway
Individual progression rates vary widely and are poorly predicted
Role of non-H. pylori microbiome in cascade progression only recently explored
